text: The surgeon introduces the optical portal using the rule of the palm, measuring approximately seventeen and a half centimeters from the palm to the end of the index finger.
The surgeon uses an atraumatic trachea to penetrate the abdominal cavity, preparing for further surgical steps in the gastrectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses a camera to visualize the abdominal cavity, initiating insufflation with a standard setting of fourteen to fifteen millimeters of mercury pressure.
The surgeon confirms entry into the abdominal cavity following insufflation and secures the area with a surgical napkin.
The surgeon applies traction to the stomach, visibly stretching it in the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon places a twelve-millimeter port slightly lateral to the sternocleidomastoid line, preparing for the use of staplers. Another working port is positioned near the projection of the duodenum.

The surgical team positions the fifth port for the assistant, likely to be used for drainage, and places a sub-xiphoidal port. The standard port locations for the gastrectomy are shown, including the optical port at the first stage of the operation. The patient is slightly tilted to the left in the Fowler position, with visible anatomical landmarks such as the left rib and sub-xiphoidal area marked for port placement.

The surgeon approaches the sixth group of lymph nodes, carefully avoiding opening the mesocolon, which appears slightly adhered. The assistant provides traction on the stomach using one hand while demonstrating the surgical field with the other.
The surgeon navigates into the layer of fusion fascia, approaching the sixth group of lymph nodes while avoiding the mesocolon.
The surgeon requests the harmonica instrument to be cleaned. The pancreas is visible in the surgical field, with the camera moving slightly to the right for a closer view.
The surgeon adjusts the view to focus closely on the pancreas, highlighting its anatomical position and details.

The surgeon uses a dissector to carefully manipulate tissue near the pancreas, ensuring not to break it. The tip of the instrument is shown as clips are applied, with one clip being secured with a gasket.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to address a torn clip below the surgical field. The camera focuses on a node, initially unclear if it is an artery or a node, but confirmed as a node. The sixth group of lymph nodes is visible in the surgical area.
The surgeon positions scissors to protect the stomach with a safe branch, preparing to mobilize the tissue to the left. The device is placed under the tissue for stabilization.
The camera focuses on a lymph node located towards the back of the surgical field, with the surgeon considering moving closer for a better view.

The surgeon observes venous flow and waits for fifteen seconds for fluid release from the tissue, followed by another fifteen seconds for clip deformation. A napkin is placed to manage the situation after a movement caused tearing of short veins near the pancreaticoduodenal area.
The surgeon manipulates the tissue, revealing the right diaphragm and a visible leg structure, while adjusting the view for closer inspection.

The surgeon uses the epigastric port to navigate around the lymphatic tissue, ensuring not to disturb the lymph node itself. The surgical field includes the thirteenth node, with adjustments made to the position of a napkin for better visibility and control.
The camera moves closer to reveal the edge of the pancreas. The surgical assistant cleans the harmonica instrument with water.

The surgeon requests a desector and focuses on the cartilaginous vein and artery, attempting to work on the eleventh group of lymph nodes. The team is advised to handle the clips gently to avoid breaking them.
The surgeon performs a subtotal gastrectomy, removing the gland along the lesser curvature to complete the lymph node dissection.
The surgical team adjusts the view to show a panoramic view of the surgical field, highlighting the contour of the transverse bladder.

The surgeon completes the gastrectomy and examines the surgical field. The stomach is packed, and the surgeon inspects the area, identifying the vein and salivary artery. The eleventh P group of vessels is visible, while the seventh, eighth, and sixth vessels are missing. The twelfth vessel is labeled as A, and the central section with a small curve is noted.

The surgical team tilts the patient to the right, repositioning the optical port to the right side in the epigastric region. The operating surgeon and assistant move to the opposite side to continue the procedure.

The surgical team prepares to dissect the tenth group of lymph nodes, adjusting the patient's position for optimal access. The Mezokolani window is visible, indicating the area where the dissection will occur. Instruments are positioned carefully to avoid tearing tissue, and the surgeon identifies the location of the trades.

The surgeon requests the harmonic scalpel and creates a window at the tracer connection site. The assistant stretches the tissue closer to the camera, preparing to stretch the RU loop. The team measures approximately twenty-five centimeters from the ligament of the tracer for precise placement.
The surgeon examines the arcade of vessels, highlighting the need to disengage two loops that do not cross, focusing on the actual blood supply. The vessel is clearly visible in the surgical field.

The surgical team manipulates a loop of tissue, with Dima adjusting its position upwards and to the right. The team prepares to ligate the structure, with Dima holding it steady. A request is made to drip Flouran and clear the area.

The surgical team prepares to place the anastomosis, adjusting the position of the surrounding tissue to avoid interference. The surgeon cuts off a corner of the tissue to facilitate the start of the anastomosis placement.
The surgical device is introduced through the right portal in the Podriberi region, with optical visualization. The surgeon adjusts the device's position for closer examination.

The surgeon manipulates the stomach, preserving the vascular arcade at the posterior aspect. Instruments are used to hold and adapt the stomach's position. A branch is picked up for further surgical action.
The surgeon requests scissors while adjusting the position of a wrapped structure, bringing it closer into view for examination.
The surgeon cuts the thread and lowers the clamp, using two clamps to prepare the second wall for further surgical manipulation.

The surgeon aligns the surgical field from left to right and proceeds to the outer stage of the procedure. A limited central laparotomy is performed, with a wound protector placed to facilitate extraction and creation of an anastomosis. The incision measures approximately five to six centimeters. The top light is turned on, and hooks are used to manipulate the tissue.
The surgeon demonstrates the completed side-by-side cassette anastomosis and proceeds to close the technological hole using an acrylic seam.
The surgeon, Dmitry Andreevich, uses his fingers to manipulate the gut and carefully knits with a needle holder, efficiently managing the thread during the suturing process.

The surgeon fixes the edges where the stapler line begins to ensure there is no tension on the stem of the stapler. The probe is adjusted slightly at a height of fifteen centimeters below the anastomosis.
